claimed_contribution: Introduces a new associate editor (AE) reviewing system for Journal of Marketing Research, effective April 1, 2006, under editor Joel Huber.
Explains why an AE structure is needed for JMR given increasing specialization in marketing scholarship and greater complexity in marketing practice.
Clarifies process implications for stakeholders, including author AE suggestions, editor-assigned AEs, and final editorial decisions based on AE and reviewer input.
context: The note concerns the editorial review process at Journal of Marketing Research (JMR), an American Marketing Association journal. It situates JMR against other marketing journals that already use AE-based review systems.
design_and_data: This is an editorial policy note, not an empirical study. It describes a procedural change to JMR's manuscript review system and outlines how decisions will be made (editor plus input from two reviewers and one AE). The note also presents a roster of 12 newly appointed AEs and their areas of expertise and managerial focus.
focal_constructs: topic diversity and depth in published research
main_findings: JMR adopted an AE-based reviewing system as of April 1, 2006.
The editor retains final manuscript decisions at each round, informed by two reviewers and the assigned AE.
The note argues that using AEs should improve mentoring and allow broader, deeper, and more innovative coverage of marketing topics in JMR.
one_line_summary: This editorial note announces and justifies JMR's shift to an associate editor review structure and explains how it changes manuscript handling for authors and readers.
relation_to_prior_work: introduces an associate editor structure for JMR in place of the traditional AMA approach without AEs, while aligning JMR more closely with journals such as Marketing Science and Journal of Consumer Research that already use AE systems.
research_gap: The note identifies a mismatch between the traditional single-editor AMA review model and the growing specialization and complexity of modern marketing research and practice. It also highlights the risk that rotating editors can cause journal focus to shift with each editor's disciplinary background.
research_question: How should JMR redesign its reviewing process to handle increasingly specialized and diverse marketing scholarship while maintaining quality and coherence? What are the practical implications of moving to an AE-based system for manuscript evaluation and journal content?
